content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 23, 2004

Michael D. Milich
City of Modesto
Office of the City Attorney
Post Office Box 642
Modesto, CA 95353

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-216

Dear Mr. Milich:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Firoz Vohra for advice
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹
Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained
herein apply only to prospective actions. Our advice is based on the facts presented in
your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In
re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

May Mr. Vohra participate in or influence decisions related to the following
roadway construction projects when those projects are now mitigating measures under
the CEQA law for the Kaiser Project and Mr. Vohra's wife works for Kaiser:

• State Route 219 Widening and Intersection Improvements

• Pelandale Interchange Improvements

• Pelandale Widening from Dale Road to McHenry

• Dale Road Widening and Improvements

_______________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. I-04-216
Page No. 2

• Intersection Improvements at Bangs and American

• Improvements to the Intersection of Bangs and Prescott?

CONCLUSIONS

Mr. Vohra may not participate in decisions if they relate to the Kaiser Project.
However, he may participate in the specified roadway decisions so long as the projects
are separable from the Kaiser Project and the roadway projects will not have a
foreseeable and material financial effect on his economic interests.

FACTS

Firoz Vohra serves as the deputy director of the city's public works department.
He is in charge of the city's traffic engineering/planning division and its development
services division. His primary expertise is in traffic engineering.

Mr. Vohra has been the city's lead contact on most discussions with Caltrans, and
he is also responsible for processing new development projects, particularly relating to
the infrastructure of the city.

The Kaiser Project has far-reaching impacts to traffic circulation in northwest
Modesto. There is substantial crossover between existing and future capital improvement
projects, which would have been completed without the Kaiser Project. Those same
projects are now identified as mitigation measures under the CEQA law for the Kaiser
Project.

The County LAFCO has approved the Kaiser property annexation to the city, and
decisions about mitigation measures have been made. Accordingly, the city is now in
the design and implementation phases of the Kaiser Project. As the mitigations are very
extensive, you require clarification of capital improvement projects in the area of the
Kaiser Project on which Mr. Vohra can now work.

Mr. Vohra's home is located in a subdivision "next door" to the Kaiser Hospital
Project, and the subdivision is within 500 feet of the Kaiser Project. However, his spouse
is an employee of Kaiser Hospital.

Pursuant to our phone conversation, you advised that Mr. Vohra's home is located
in a subdivision next door to the Kaiser Project and the boundaries of the home are 725
feet from the Kaiser Project. In addition, none of the roadway mitigation projects are
within 500 feet of Mr. Vohra's home. You also advised that the homeowners association
of the subdivision where Mr. Vohra's real property is located is concerned about the
effects of the Kaiser Project on their homes.

File No. 1-04-216
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Under certain circumstances, a public official disqualified from one decision may
participate in other related decisions, provided that the official's participation in the latter
does not affect the decision in which he or she has a disqualifying conflict of interest.
(Sweeney Advice Letter, No. A-89-639.) To determine if Mr. Vohra may participate in
any of the above decisions you must first determine if the roadway mitigation projects
can be segmented or separated from the Kaiser Project. If the roadway projects are
interrelated to another decision in which Mr.Vohra has a conflict of interest, those
decisions must be decided separately in accordance with the commission's segmentation
process under regulation 18709. Each of the governmental decisions should be analyzed
independently to determine whether there will be a foreseeable and material financial
effect on the public official's economic interests. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.)

Regulation 18709 provides, in pertinent part:

"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all
of the following conditions apply:

(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated
to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;

(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way; and

(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest
has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not
result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from
which the official was disqualified.

(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are 'inextricably
interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision."

If Mr. Vohra determines that section 18709 applies in that any or all of the
projects can be segmented from the Kaiser Project, the following conflict-of-interest
analysis would then apply to each of those decisions.

File No. 1-04-216
Page No. 4

Conflict-of-Interest Analysis

The primary purpose of the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)

In furtherance of this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official
from making, participating in making or otherwise using his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The
Commission has adopted an eight-step analysis for deciding whether an official has a
disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Steps One and Two: Is Deputy Director Vohra a public official making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. As the
Deputy Director of Public Works for the City of Modesto, Mr. Vohra is a "member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency" and is, therefore, a
public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048;
regulation 18701(a).)

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; regulation 18702.1.)

In addition, a public official "participates in making a governmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant
substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Section
87100; regulation 18702.2.) A public official attempts to use his or her official position
to influence a decision before his or her own agency if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of the agency. (Section 87100; regulation 18702.3.)

Thus, if Mr. Vohra were to advise or represent the city on issues related to the
Kaiser project, he would be participating in making or influencing a governmental
decision.

Step Three: What are Mr. Vohra's economic interests – the possible sources of a
conflict of interest?

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to conflicts resulting from certain
specified economic interests. These economic interests are defined in section 87103 and
regulations 18703-18703.5.

File No. I-04-216
Page No. 5

"(a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section
87103(a), regulation 18703.1(a).)

"(b) Any real property in which the public official has a direct or
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section
87103(b), regulation 18703.2.)

"(c) Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial
lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating five
hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received
by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made. (Section 87103(c), regulation 18703.3.)

"(d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d), regulation 18703.1(b).)

"(e) Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a
gift or gifts aggregating [three hundred forty dollars ($340)]² or more in
value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section 87103(e),
regulation 18703.4.)

"(f) Any effect on the personal finances of the elected official or
those of his or her immediate family, is a personal financial effect.
(Section 87103, regulation 18703.5.)

Mr. Vohra may have a conflict of interest resulting from two of his economic
interests. First, assuming Mr. Vohra has an ownership interest of $2,000 or more in his
home, he has an economic interest in this real property. Secondly, Mr. Vohra has an
economic interest in Kaiser Hospital as a source of income because his wife receives a
salary from Kaiser, assuming that the one-half portion of the income attributed to him
aggregates to five hundred dollars or more within 12 months prior to the governmental
decision.³

You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests
Mr. Vohra may have. For purposes of this letter, we assume that he has no other
economic interests relevant to the decision you have identified.

_________________________________

² This figure is adjusted biennially to reflect changes in the Consumer Price Index. (Regulation
18940.2.)
³ Income includes "any community property interest in the income of a spouse." (Section 82030.)

File No. I-04-216
Page No. 6


Step Four: Are Mr. Vohra's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?

Real Property: Real property in which a public official has an economic interest
is directly involved in a governmental decision if, among other things:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision….

"(2) The governmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a
similar decision affecting the real property. For purposes of this
subdivision, the terms 'zoning' and 'rezoning' shall refer to the act of
establishing or changing the zoning or land use designation on the real
property in which the official has an interest.

"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or
revocation of a license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a
specific use or uses of the real property in which the official has an
interest.

"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or
modification of any taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property
in which the official has an interest.

"(5) The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.

"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."

File No. I-04-216
Page No. 7

Mr. Vohra's real property is not located within 500 feet of any of the projects
(roadway projects or the Kaiser Project) involved in the governmental decisions.
Therefore, his property would be indirectly involved in the decision.⁴

Source of Income: For governmental decisions that affect sources of income, the
standards set forth in regulation 18704.1(a) apply.

Regulation 18704.1 states:

"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:

(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or:

(2) Is a named party in or is the subject of, the proceeding
concerning the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

If Kaiser Hospital initiated the roadway project, is a named party in the project
(such as with respect to the Kaiser Project), or is otherwise the subject of a proceeding,
Kaiser Hospital would be directly involved in the governmental decision. Otherwise it is
considered to be indirectly involved.

Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?

Real Property: "The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property
which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in
which the public official has an interest." (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)

Under this standard a decision's financial effect on the official's economic
interest is presumed not to be material. This presumption can be rebutted by proof that
there are specific circumstances when the official's real property could be impacted by
the governmental decision. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:

⁴ However, if Mr. Vohra's real property were to receive new or improved services, such as
construction of improvements to streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, then his real
property may be directly involved in the governmental decision under regulation 18704.2(a)(6). Based
upon the facts we cannot determine if this provision applies.

File No. 1-04-216
Page No. 8

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;

(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;

(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (Regulation
18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

Mr. Vohra will need to determine whether the roadway projects will have a
material financial effect on his real property based upon any of the above factors or any
additional factors that could affect the value of his real property.

Sources of Income: Regulation 18705.3(a) sets forth the materiality standard for
directly involved economic interests in persons who are sources of income. It states that
"[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a
public official, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is
deemed material." (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

The materiality standard for an indirectly involved source of income that is a
business entity is set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)). Under that test, "the financial effect
of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $500,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more."
(Regulation 18705.1(c)(2)(A)-(C).)

Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
meet the materiality standards?

As noted above, a conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is
material. An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial
effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

File No. I-04-216
Page No. 9

Because Mr. Vohra's real property is indirectly involved in the governmental
decision it is presumed to be immaterial. You have stated that the Kaiser Project has far-
reaching impacts to traffic circulation, which could materially affect Mr. Vohra's real
property. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice.
However, if it were reasonably foreseeable that the presumption could be rebutted then
there would a disqualifying conflict of interest. Mr. Vohra will need to make the ultimate
determination on whether it is substantially likely that there is a material financial effect
on his real property. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

The standard for determining the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect on
a source of income that is directly involved is the "one penny" rule. If the income of the
business entity, in this case Kaiser Hospital, were to increase or decrease by one penny
because of the governmental decision, the economic interest is deemed material.
(Regulation 18705.3(a).) If Kaiser Hospital would be indirectly involved, it is likely the
threshold set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)(2) would be met; i.e., earnings before taxes of
no less than $2.5 million would be realized. Mr. Vohra will need to determine, based
upon the above information, if any of the roadway decisions will have a material
financial effect on Kaiser Hospital. He will need to make this determination on a case-by-
case basis with respect to the six projects you described.

Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation

Step seven applies an exception when the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 83112; regulation 18707.) Step eight applies an exception
when the official's participation in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section
87101; regulation 18708.) Both of these narrowly construed exceptions are fact-driven,
and you have provided no facts indicating that these exceptions apply here.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Kara Mazzucca
Intern, Legal Division

Enclosure

Conflict-of-Interest An alysis

impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).)

from making, participating in making or otherwise using his or her official position to

disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation I 8700(b)(l )-(8).)
Steps One and Two: Is Deputy Director Vohra a public official making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to public officials. As the

In addition, a public official "participates in making a govefllmental decision"
when, acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant

87100; regulation 18702.2.) A public official attempts to use his or her official position

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply to conflicts resulting from certain

regulations 1 8703-1 8703.5.

Mr. Vohra may have a conflict of interest resulting from two of his economlc
interests. First, assuming Mr. Vohra has an ownership interest of $2,000 or more in his

Step Four: Are Mr. Vohra's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
Real Property: Real property in which a public off,rcial has an economic interest
is directly involved in a governmental decision if, among other things:
"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. . ..
"(2) The goverrrmental decision involves the zoning or rezoning,
annexation or deannexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or
exclusion from any city, county, district or other local governmental
subdivision, of the real property in which the official has an interest or a

subdivision, the terms 'zoning' and 'rezoning' shall refer to the act of

property in which the official has an interest.
"(3) The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial or

specific use or uses of the real property in which the oflicial has an
interest.
"(4) The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or

in which the official has an interest.
"(5) The goverrlmental decision is to designate the survey area, to
select the project area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area
committee, to certify the environmental document, to adopt the
redevelopment plan, to add territory to the redevelopment area, or to
rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real property in which
the official has an interest, or any part of it is located within the
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the redevelopment area.
"(6) The decision involves construction of, or improvements to,
streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real
property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved
services."


File No. l-04-216
Mr. Vohra's real property is not located within 500 feet of any of the projects
(roadway projects or the Kaiser Project) involved in the governmental decisions.
'it 
"refoi", 
his property would be indirectly involved in the decision.a
Source of Income: For governmental decisions that affect sources of income, the
standards set forth in regulation 18704.1(a) apply.
Regulation 187 04.1 states:
"(a) A person, including business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or:
(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."
If Kaiser Hospital initiated the roadway project, is a named party in the project
(such as with respect to the Kaiser Project), or is otherwise the subject of a proceeding,
Kaiser Hospital would be directly involved in the governmental decision. Otherwise it is
considered to be indirectly involved.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
Real Property: "The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property
which is indirectly involved in the goverrrmental decision is presumed not to be material.
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the govemmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in
which the public official has an interest." (Regulation 18705.2(b)(l).)
Under this standard a decision's financial effect on the official's economic
interest is presumed not to be material. This presumption can be rebutted by proof that
there are specific circumstances when the official's real property could be impacted by
the governmental decision. Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered
include, but are not limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:
4 Ho*ever, if Mr. Vohra's real property were to receive new or improved services, such as
construction of improvements to streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, then his real
property may be directly involved in the governmental decision under regulation 18704.2(a)(6). Based
upon the facts we cannot determine if this provision applies.


File No. l-04-216
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of
the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited
to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise
levels, air emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (Regulation
1 870s.2(b)( 1 XA)-(c).)
Mr. Vohra will need to determine whether the roadway projects will have a
material financial effect on his real property based upon any of the above factors or any
additional factors that could aft-ect the value of his real property.
Sources of Income: Regulation 18705.3(a) sets forth the materiality standard for
directly involved economic interests in persons who are sources of income. It states that
"[a]ny reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source of income to a
public offrcial, and who is directly involved in a decision before the official's agency, is
deemed material." (Regulation 1 8705.3(a).)
The materiality standard for an indirectly involved source of income that is a
business entity is set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)). Under that test, "the financial effect
of a governmental decision on the business entity is material if it is reasonably
foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease to the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the
amount of $500,000 or more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $200,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more."
(Regulation 1 870s. 1 (c)(2XA)-(C).)
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will
meet the materiality standards?
As noted above, a conflict of interest arises only rvhen the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interests is
material. An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial
effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)


File No. l-04-216
Because Mr. Vohra's real property is indirectly involved in the governmental
decision it is presumed to be immaterial. You have stated that the Kaiser Project has far-
reaching impacts to traffic circulation, which could materially affect Mr. Vohra's real
property. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice.
However, if it were reasonably foreseeable that the presumption could be rebutted then
there would a disqualifyng conflict of interest. Mr. Vohra will need to make the ultimate
determination on whether it is substantially likely that there is a material financial effect
on his real property. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
The standard for determining the reasonable foreseeability of a financial effect on
a source of income that is directly involved is the "one panny''rule. If the income of the
business entity, in this case Kaiser Hospital, were to increase or decrease by one penny
because of the governmental decision, the economic interest is deerned material.
(Regulation 18705.3(a).) If Kaiser Hospital would be indirectly involved, it is likely the
threshold set forth in regulation 18705.1(c)(2) would be met; i.e., earnings before taxes of
no less than $2.5 million would be realized. Mr. Vohra will need to determine, based
upon the above information, if any of the roadway decisions will have a material
financial effect on Kaiser Hospital. He will need to make this determination on a case-by-
case basis with respect to the six projects you described.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation
Step seven applies an exception when the reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on an official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally. (Section 831121, regulation 18707.) Step eight applies an exception
when the official's participation in a governmental decision is legally required. (Section
87101; regulation 18708.) Both of these narrowly construed exceptions are fact-driven,
and you have provided no facts indicating that these exceptions apply here.

CONCLUSION: Mr. Vohra may not participate in decisions if they relate to the Kaiser Project.
However, he may participate in the specified roadway decisions so long as the projects

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 November 23, 2004 Michael D. Milich City of Modesto Office of the City Attorney Post Office Box 642 Modesto, CA 95353 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-04-216 Dear Mr. Milich: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Firoz Vohra for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A), copy enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained herein apply only to prospective actions. Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS May Mr. Vohra participate in or influence decisions related to the following roadway construction projects when those projects are now mitigating measures under the CEQA law for the Kaiser Project and Mr. Vohra's wife works for Kaiser: • State Route 219 Widening and Intersection Improvements • Pelandale Interchange Improvements • Pelandale Widening from Dale Road to McHenry • Dale Road Widening and Improvements _______________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. I-04-216 Page No. 2 • Intersection Improvements at Bangs and American • Improvements to the Intersection of Bangs and Prescott? CONCLUSIONS Mr. Vohra may not participate in decisions if they relate to the Kaiser Project. However, he may participate in the specified roadway decisions so long as the projects are separable from the Kaiser Project and the roadway projects will not have a foreseeable and material financial effect on his economic interests. FACTS Firoz Vohra serves as the deputy director of the city's public works department. He is in charge of the city's traffic engineering/planning division and its development services division. His primary expertise is in traffic engineering. Mr. Vohra has been the city's lead contact on most discussions with Caltrans, and he is also responsible for processing new development projects, particularly relating to the infrastructure of the city. The Kaiser Project has far-reaching impacts to traffic circulation in northwest Modesto. There is substantial crossover between existing and future capital improvement projects, which would have been completed without the Kaiser Project. Those same projects are now identified as mitigation measures under the CEQA law for the Kaiser Project. The County LAFCO has approved the Kaiser property annexation to the city, and decisions about mitigation measures have been made. Accordingly, the city is now in the design and implementation phases of the Kaiser Project. As the mitigations are very extensive, you require clarification of capital improvement projects in the area of the Kaiser Project on which Mr. Vohra can now work. Mr. Vohra's home is located
